note_data.title: Electrochemistry
keyPoints: Compound - 2 or more elements chemically joined together - Contains Covalent or Ionic Bonds
Mixtures - 2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together - Can consist of Elements, Compounds, or Elements + Compounds
Pure Substances have: Fixed composition, Fixed Mp/Bp, Produces only 1 spot on chromatogram, All molecules same
Mixtures have: Variable Composition, Variable Bp/Mp, 2 or more spots on Chromatogram, 2 or more diff. molecules
Insoluble solid & Liquid → Filtration - Liquid particles small enough to go thru filter paper pores, Solid particles too large
Solid & Liquid solution → Crystallization (Obtaining solid) - Formation of crystals from a cooling liquid/Saturated solution
Solid & Liquid solution → Distillation (Obtaining Liquid) - Pure liquid change to gas easily – Low BP, Solid does not boil – High BP
2 Liquids mixed (Miscible) → Fractional Distillation - Liquid with lowest BP comes out 1st
Mixture of Organic Compounds → Chromatography (Eg. Colour Dyes)

definitions.definition: no more solid can dissolve in it

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a detailed account including what happens at each electrode
Use discharge rules to determine products at each electrode

modelAnswer.question: Describe what happens during the electrolysis of dilute sulfuric acid using platinum electrodes. Include the electrode reactions.
modelAnswer.answer: Water molecules are decomposed. At the cathode, hydrogen ions gain electrons to form hydrogen gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂. At the anode, hydroxide ions lose electrons to form oxygen gas and water: 4OH⁻ → O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻. Hydrogen gas is produced at the cathode and oxygen gas is produced at the anode. The volume of hydrogen is twice the volume of oxygen. The solution becomes more concentrated as water is removed.

commonMistakes: Confusing compounds and mixtures - compounds are chemically joined, mixtures are not
Not understanding that pure substances have fixed composition and melting/boiling points while mixtures have variable properties
Mixing up which separation method to use - filtration for insoluble solids, crystallization to obtain solid from solution, distillation to obtain liquid from solution
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystalisation'
The constant temperature of distillation process = Boiling Point of pure liquid
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Electrolysis cell showing two electrodes in electrolyte, with battery/power supply, electron flow from anode to cathode through external circuit, and ion movement in solution (cations to cathode, anions to anode)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in past papers asking about electrolysis of molten magnesium chloride — students needed to identify that Mg²⁺ gains electrons at cathode to form magnesium metal, while Cl⁻ loses electrons at anode to form chlorine gas
Aluminium extraction questions appear regularly — candidates must explain that cryolite lowers melting point of aluminium oxide and increases conductivity, making the process more economical
Electrolysis of concentrated aqueous sodium chloride is frequently examined — students need to distinguish between products from molten vs aqueous solutions and understand selective discharge

Questions on aqueous magnesium iodide electrolysis test understanding of preferential discharge — hydrogen forms at cathode (not magnesium) due to reactivity series, iodine forms at anode as halides are preferentially discharged over hydroxide ions

workedExample.question: Explain the electrolysis of aqueous copper(II) sulfate using copper electrodes. State the observations and write ionic equations for the reactions at both electrodes. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify ions present - Cu²⁺, SO₄²⁻, H⁺, OH⁻
Step 2: At cathode (negative): Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper deposited, electrode gains mass)
Step 3: At anode (positive): Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ (copper dissolves, electrode loses mass)
Step 4: Overall effect: copper transfers from anode to cathode, solution concentration stays constant
workedExample.answer: Cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper deposits, electrode becomes heavier and pinker). Anode: Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ (copper dissolves, electrode becomes lighter). The blue color of copper sulfate solution remains unchanged as copper concentration stays constant.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for correct cathode and anode equations, 1 mark each for observations at electrodes, 1 mark for explaining color stays same, 1 mark for mentioning mass changes

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the products of electrolysis of molten compounds versus aqueous solutions' — always consider water's involvement in aqueous electrolysis and use reactivity series for metal discharge

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently wrote molecular equations instead of ionic equations for electrode reactions' — always show electron transfer clearly using ionic half-equations with state symbols

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often failed to explain why certain ions are preferentially discharged' — mention reactivity series for cations and halide preference for anions, plus concentration effects
note_data.keyDiagram: Electrolysis setup showing: (1) Power supply with labeled positive and negative terminals, (2) Two inert electrodes (graphite/platinum) labeled as anode (+) and cathode (-), (3) Electrolyte solution/molten compound with ions moving toward opposite electrodes, (4) Arrows showing electron flow in external circuit from anode to cathode, (5) Gas collection tubes if gases are produced, (6) Clear labeling of all products formed at each electrode
note_data.quickRecap: Compounds are elements chemically joined together while mixtures are substances not chemically joined. Pure substances have fixed properties while mixtures have variable properties. Different separation methods are used based on the type of mixture: filtration for insoluble solids, crystallization to obtain solids, distillation to obtain liquids, fractional distillation for miscible liquids, and chromatography for organic compounds.
